name: Shell Completions, Help & Integration Tests
theme: Polish and professional finish — completions, help text, and real-world testing.

criteria.description: 'mailctl --help' output lists both '--install-completion' and '--show-completion' as available options
testSteps: Run 'mailctl --help' via CliRunner or subprocess
Verify stdout contains '--install-completion'
Verify stdout contains '--show-completion'
criteria.threshold: Both --install-completion and --show-completion visible in --help

criteria.description: 'mailctl --show-completion zsh' outputs a non-empty shell script containing zsh completion syntax (e.g., compdef, _arguments, or _mailctl)
testSteps: Run 'mailctl --show-completion zsh' or equivalent Typer completion output mechanism
Verify stdout is non-empty (at least 10 lines)
Verify stdout contains zsh-specific completion syntax (compdef or _mailctl or compadd or similar)
criteria.threshold: Non-empty zsh completion script output containing zsh completion keywords

criteria.description: The zsh completion script generated by --show-completion includes references to all top-level commands and subcommand groups: accounts, mailboxes, messages, drafts, compose, reply, forward, doctor
testSteps: Capture the output of 'mailctl --show-completion zsh'
Verify the script text contains 'accounts', 'mailboxes', 'messages', 'drafts', 'compose', 'reply', 'forward', and 'doctor'
criteria.threshold: All 8 command names present in completion script

criteria.description: 'mailctl --install-completion zsh' exits cleanly (exit 0 or prints a message about completion installation) and does not error out
testSteps: Run 'mailctl --install-completion zsh' via subprocess
Verify exit code is 0
Verify stderr does not contain traceback or error
criteria.threshold: Exit 0 with no traceback/error

criteria.description: Every Typer command in the CLI has a non-empty help string. Specifically: accounts list, mailboxes list, messages list, messages show, messages search, messages mark, messages move, messages delete, compose, reply, forward, drafts edit, and doctor — 13 commands total, each with help text visible in '--help' output
testSteps: Run '--help' for each of the 13 commands listed
Verify each produces a description line (not just 'Usage:' with no description)
Verify no help output says 'No help text provided' or is blank after the command name
criteria.threshold: All 13 commands have non-empty help descriptions

criteria.description: Every option defined across all commands has a non-empty help string. Spot-check at least 5 commands — verify that each option listed in their '--help' output shows a description (not just the flag name). Focus on: messages list (7+ options), compose (8+ options), messages mark (4+ options), messages search (6+ options), doctor (2+ options)
testSteps: Run '--help' for messages list, compose, messages mark, messages search, doctor
For each command, verify that every option line (lines starting with -- or containing --) has descriptive text after the flag name
No option should appear with only its flag name and no description
criteria.threshold: All options in the 5 spot-checked commands have help descriptions

criteria.description: The help text for compose, reply, and forward commands explicitly mentions that the default behaviour creates a draft (not sends). The word 'draft' appears in the command-level help or in the command description visible in '--help'
testSteps: Run 'mailctl compose --help' and verify 'draft' appears in the output
Run 'mailctl reply --help' and verify 'draft' appears in the output
Run 'mailctl forward --help' and verify 'draft' appears in the output
criteria.threshold: 'draft' mentioned in help for all three compose/reply/forward commands

criteria.description: The help text for the --dangerously-send option (on compose, reply, forward) clearly warns that this will actually send the email. The help string for this option contains 'send' and conveys the destructive nature (e.g., 'Actually send' or 'Send the message' or warning language)
testSteps: Run 'mailctl compose --help' and find the --dangerously-send option
Verify the help text for --dangerously-send contains 'send' and conveys it performs a real send (not just draft)
Run 'mailctl reply --help' and verify the same for --dangerously-send
Run 'mailctl forward --help' and verify the same for --dangerously-send
criteria.threshold: --dangerously-send help text warns about sending in all three commands

criteria.description: 'mailctl --help' produces a clean overview listing all command groups (accounts, mailboxes, messages, drafts) and all top-level commands (compose, reply, forward, doctor) with one-line descriptions for each
testSteps: Run 'mailctl --help'
Verify output contains all 8 entries: accounts, mailboxes, messages, drafts, compose, reply, forward, doctor
Verify each entry has visible description text (not just the command name)
criteria.threshold: All 8 commands/groups listed with descriptions in mailctl --help

criteria.description: Help text style is consistent across commands: command-level descriptions use sentence case (first word capitalised, rest lowercase unless proper noun), and end with a period or are single-phrase imperatives. No ALL-CAPS descriptions, no descriptions that start with lowercase
testSteps: Inspect all help= strings in command registration calls across all command modules
Verify each starts with an uppercase letter
Verify none is ALL-CAPS (entire string)
Verify style is consistent (all imperative 'List messages...' or all descriptive 'Lists messages...' — not mixed)
criteria.threshold: All command help strings start uppercase, consistent style, no ALL-CAPS

criteria.description: Options with default values include the default in their help text. Spot-check: messages list --limit, messages list --mailbox, messages search --limit — each mentions its default value in the help string
testSteps: Run 'mailctl messages list --help' and check --limit help text mentions 25 (or the actual default)
Check --mailbox help text mentions INBOX (or the actual default)
Run 'mailctl messages search --help' and check --limit help text mentions its default
criteria.threshold: At least 3 options with defaults show their default values in help text

criteria.description: Integration test files exist in tests/integration/ (not just __init__.py). At least one file matching test_*.py or *_test.py exists. All test functions in integration test files are decorated with @pytest.mark.integration
testSteps: Inspect tests/integration/ for Python test files
Verify at least one test_*.py file exists with test functions
Verify all test functions (or the module/class) use the @pytest.mark.integration marker
criteria.threshold: At least one integration test file with @pytest.mark.integration on all tests

criteria.description: An integration test for 'mailctl accounts list' exists that runs against real Mail.app, verifies exit code 0, and verifies the output contains at least one account. This is a read-only operation.
testSteps: Run integration tests with 'pytest -m integration tests/integration/ -v'
Verify the accounts-list integration test passes
Inspect the test to confirm it invokes 'mailctl accounts list' (or equivalent) and asserts on exit code and output
criteria.threshold: accounts list integration test passes against real Mail.app

criteria.description: An integration test for 'mailctl mailboxes list' exists that runs against real Mail.app, verifies exit code 0, and verifies the output contains at least one mailbox (e.g., INBOX). Read-only operation.
testSteps: Run the mailboxes-list integration test
Verify it passes
Inspect the test to confirm it checks for at least one mailbox in the output
criteria.threshold: mailboxes list integration test passes against real Mail.app

criteria.description: An integration test for 'mailctl messages list' exists that runs against real Mail.app, targeting INBOX, verifies exit code 0. Read-only operation. The test handles both the case where INBOX has messages and where it's empty (both should succeed).
testSteps: Run the messages-list integration test
Verify it passes
Inspect the test to confirm it targets INBOX and handles both non-empty and empty results gracefully
criteria.threshold: messages list integration test passes against real Mail.app

criteria.description: An integration test for 'mailctl doctor' exists that runs against real Mail.app, verifies exit code 0 (all checks pass on the test machine), and verifies output contains pass indicators for all checks. Read-only operation.
testSteps: Run the doctor integration test
Verify it passes with exit code 0
Verify the test checks that doctor output shows passing checks
criteria.threshold: doctor integration test passes against real Mail.app

criteria.description: An integration test for 'mailctl accounts list --json' and 'mailctl mailboxes list --json' verifies that --json output from real Mail.app is valid JSON and has the expected structure (list of objects with expected keys). Read-only operation.
testSteps: Run the JSON-output integration test(s)
Verify the test parses the output as JSON successfully
Verify the parsed JSON has the expected structure (e.g., list of dicts for accounts)
criteria.threshold: JSON output integration tests parse valid JSON with expected structure

criteria.description: Running 'pytest' from the project root (with default addopts) collects and runs unit tests but does NOT run integration tests. The integration tests are excluded by the default marker filter in pyproject.toml
testSteps: Run 'pytest -v' from the project root without any -m flag
Verify that no test from tests/integration/ appears in the collected tests
Verify that unit tests still run and pass
criteria.threshold: Default pytest run excludes integration tests

criteria.description: Running 'pytest -m integration' from the project root collects and runs ONLY integration tests. The marker filter works in both directions — default excludes them, explicit -m includes only them.
testSteps: Run 'pytest -m integration tests/integration/ -v'
Verify that integration tests are collected and run
Verify at least 5 integration test functions are collected
criteria.threshold: pytest -m integration runs at least 5 integration tests

criteria.description: tests/integration/ contains documentation (README.md or a prominent module-level docstring in conftest.py or __init__.py) explaining: (a) how to run integration tests, (b) that they require Mail.app to be running, (c) that they are read-only / safe, (d) that they are skipped by default
testSteps: Check for tests/integration/README.md or a detailed docstring in tests/integration/conftest.py or __init__.py
Verify it explains the 4 required points: how to run, Mail.app requirement, safety/read-only nature, skipped by default
criteria.threshold: Documentation exists covering all 4 points

criteria.description: Integration tests use appropriate timeouts for real Mail.app communication. Either the tests set a custom timeout on subprocess calls (e.g., timeout=30), or they use pytest-timeout or a similar mechanism to prevent hanging indefinitely if Mail.app is unresponsive
testSteps: Inspect integration test code for timeout handling
Verify that subprocess calls include a timeout parameter, OR pytest-timeout is configured, OR individual tests have timeout markers
Timeout should be at least 10 seconds (to allow real Mail.app response) and no more than 60 seconds
criteria.threshold: Timeout mechanism present in integration tests (10-60 seconds range)

criteria.description: Full unit test suite (pytest from project root, default markers) passes with zero failures and zero errors. This includes all Sprint 1-9 unit tests and any new Sprint 10 unit tests
testSteps: Run 'pytest -v' from the project root
Verify all tests pass (0 failures, 0 errors)
Verify test count is at least as many as Sprint 9 had (no tests removed)
criteria.threshold: All unit tests pass, exit code 0, no test count regression

criteria.description: All Sprint 1-9 commands remain functional: 'mailctl --version' outputs the version, 'mailctl --help' lists all command groups (accounts, mailboxes, messages, drafts, compose, reply, forward, doctor), and the CLI entry point works via 'python -m mailctl' or direct invocation
testSteps: Run 'mailctl --version' and verify it outputs a version string
Run 'mailctl --help' and verify all 8 commands/groups are listed
Verify the CLI still loads without import errors
criteria.threshold: --version works, --help lists all commands, no import errors

criteria.description: The safety model is unchanged: compose, reply, and forward still default to creating drafts. The --dangerously-send flag is still the only path to sending. The Sprint 5 safety model unit tests all pass without modification
testSteps: Run the safety model tests (tests/unit/test_safety_model.py or equivalent)
Verify all pass
Verify no test in test_safety_model.py has been modified or removed in this sprint
criteria.threshold: All safety model tests pass unchanged

outOfScope: Dynamic shell completions for account/mailbox names — the spec says 'where feasible' for completing account/mailbox names; Typer's built-in completion covers commands and options. Dynamic completions requiring osascript calls at completion time are deferred as they add complexity and latency to the shell completion path
Bash/fish completion scripts — the spec targets zsh; other shells are out of scope for v1
Integration tests for write operations (compose, reply, forward, mark, move, delete) — only read-only commands are safe to test against real Mail.app without risk of side effects
Integration tests for --dangerously-send — sending real emails is absolutely forbidden per CLAUDE.md
Performance benchmarking of real Mail.app commands — integration tests verify correctness, not performance
Man page generation — the spec does not require man pages; --help is the documentation surface
Comprehensive help text rewriting — this sprint reviews and polishes help text but does not redesign the CLI's information architecture

safetyNotes.integrationTestsSafeByDesign: All integration tests are read-only: accounts list, mailboxes list, messages list, messages search, doctor. They never create, modify, move, or delete any message or draft. They never invoke --dangerously-send.
safetyNotes.completionsAreReadOnly: --install-completion and --show-completion only generate/install shell scripts. They do not interact with Mail.app or execute AppleScript.
safetyNotes.helpTextChangesAreCosmetic: Help text improvements are string-only changes to Typer help= parameters. They do not alter command behaviour, safety model, or exit codes.
safetyNotes.evalTestingSafety: The evaluator MAY run all integration tests against real Mail.app — they are entirely read-only. The evaluator MAY run 'mailctl --show-completion zsh' and 'mailctl --install-completion zsh' safely. The evaluator MUST NOT run any compose/reply/forward command against real Mail.app without mocks.
